My journey has been a bit unusual — I started out as a child chess prodigy, then moved into video game design at Bullfrog with Peter Molyneux, before pursuing a PhD in cognitive neuroscience at UCL, and ultimately founding DeepMind in 2010 with the mission of solving intelligence and then using it to solve everything else. It's a profound privilege to be working at the frontier of AI research and trying to use it to accelerate scientific discovery for the benefit of humanity.

What shaped you into who you are today?

That's a great question. I think the combination of several things really shaped my path. Growing up, I was deeply passionate about games — I was a chess prodigy, and that taught me a lot about strategic thinking and the beauty of complex problem-solving. That naturally led me into game design at Bullfrog with Peter Molyneux when I was quite young, which gave me this appreciation for building systems and simulating worlds.

That's what drew me to study neuroscience at UCL, because I genuinely believed that understanding how the brain works would give us crucial insights into building more capable AI systems. The key is to not be emotionally attached to your hypotheses. Be attached to the truth, not to being right. That's easier said than done, of course, but I think it's the most important intellectual discipline there is.

An AI that helps you think more clearly and access better information is a tremendous force for good. The incentive structures matter enormously — who is the system actually serving?

This is one of the reasons I've always argued that the development of powerful AI needs to be done responsibly, with deep thought about alignment and values. The technology itself is neutral, but the choices we make about objectives and deployment are profoundly ethical ones.

What will matter most in a hundred years?

That's the kind of question I find myself drawn to constantly. I genuinely believe that if we get AI right — and by "right" I mean both technically robust and aligned with human values — it could be the most transformative and beneficial technology in human history, accelerating scientific discovery in ways we can barely imagine today. Think about what AlphaFold did for biology in just a couple of years — now multiply that across climate science, materials science, mathematics, medicine.

But the honest answer is that what will matter most in a hundred years is whether we used this extraordinary moment wisely — whether we deployed these powerful tools to solve the great challenges facing humanity, like disease, energy, and climate change, or whether we squandered the opportunity. The science itself is neutral; it's what we choose to do with it that will define this era. I'm an optimist, but a cautious one — we have to be rigorous and responsible every step of the way.

What is unfinished?

That's a wonderfully open question. I'd say the deepest thing that's unfinished is our understanding of intelligence itself — both biological and artificial. We've made extraordinary progress with systems like AlphaFold solving protein structure prediction, but we're still in the early chapters of understanding how the brain gives rise to general cognition, and how to build AI systems that can truly reason and discover across all domains of science. The grand challenge of using AI as a tool to accelerate scientific discovery — to help us solve climate change, design new materials, find new drugs — that's profoundly unfinished, and it's what gets me most excited to come to work every day.
